metadata.summary: Disciplined platform for enterprise A/B testing with hypothesis intake, pre-registered metrics, guardrails, and SRM checks. Scales from Level 1 (ad-hoc) to Level 4 (culture-driven).

content.title: Experimentation at Scale
content.summary: Enterprise experimentation is a disciplined platform — not ad-hoc A/B testing — with defined hypothesis intake, pre-registered metrics, guardrails, SRM checks, and a learning-extraction step. Maturity runs from Level 1 (ad-hoc, no standards) to Level 4 (every decision backed by an experiment). The full agentic loop covers: hypothesis authoring → sample-size math → flag deployment → hourly SRM watch → daily metric watch → readout → cross-cutting learnings memo.

why.p: Ad-hoc A/B testing does not build organizational learning. Without statistical rigor (pre-registered primary metrics, guardrails, SRM checks, multiple-comparison correction) teams ship false winners at high rates: peeking inflates false-positive rate from 5% to 30%+; SRM breaks ~6% of experiments silently. Scale benchmarks: Microsoft 100k/yr, enterprise avg 500-1k/yr. Agents can operate the mechanical layers (collection, flag ops, SRM watch) cheaply, freeing humans for decision sign-off.

list.item: Product orgs running 50+ live A/B tests per quarter where coordination has outgrown spreadsheets
Maturity-level transitions: when formalizing hypothesis intake, sample-size math, and guardrails
Feature-flag rollouts tied to experiment readouts requiring automated SRM checks
Warehouse-native shops (Snowflake/BigQuery) driving Eppo/Statsig from dbt models
Solopreneur / small team needing an agent to generate hypotheses, monitor results, and emit readouts
Post-launch "what did we learn?" synthesis across an experiment registry
Pre-PMF with <1k weekly active users — sample-size math demands months per test; use qualitative discovery instead
Single-shot launches where a holdout is impossible or unethical
High-stakes irreversible decisions (pricing rebrand, contract terms, safety-critical UX) — use multi-method evidence, not a single A/B
Enterprise B2B with <50 accounts — sample size makes A/B impractical; use cohort analysis
Teams without a metrics governance owner (single source of truth for metric definitions)
Compliance-bound surfaces (HIPAA, payment flows, KYC) where variants must clear legal before ship

section.name: maturity-model
section.p: Level 1 (Ad-hoc): individual experiments, no standardization. Level 2 (Structured): defined process, shared tools. Level 3 (Scaled): 100+ experiments/year, statistical rigor. Level 4 (Culture): every decision backed by an experiment. Scale benchmarks: Microsoft ~100k/yr, GoDaddy 1,700+, enterprise average 500-1,000/yr.

rule.statement: Pre-register the primary metric, MDE, and decision rule before traffic flips. Agents must reject any experiment doc missing these fields.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc metric selection is the primary driver of false positives. Pre-registration reduces the false-positive rate from 30%+ (peeking) to the intended 5%.

rule.statement: Never declare a winner without: lift, two-sided CI, p-value (or posterior), pre-registered primary metric, and a passed SRM check.
rule.rationale: Each missing element introduces a separate failure mode. An LLM that paraphrases vendor statistics rather than copying them verbatim will misread CIs as posterior probabilities.

rule.statement: Implement SRM (Sample Ratio Mismatch) as a hard gate on every experiment. Chi-square check (p < 0.001) on assignment counts must pass before any readout.
rule.rationale: ~6% of experiments have SRM silently. An experiment with SRM produces bogus winner declarations that persist in institutional memory even after the bug is found.

rule.statement: Apply multiple-comparison correction (Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg) whenever more than one primary metric is analyzed. Document the correction in the decision record.
rule.rationale: 10 metrics at alpha=0.05 produces ~1 false win by chance per experiment. At scale (500/yr), this compounds to hundreds of false ships per year without correction.

rule.statement: Use CUPED for variance reduction on high-variance metrics. This cuts required sample size 30–50%. Confirm your platform supports it before planning runtime.
rule.rationale: Without variance reduction, experiments take longer, fewer run per quarter, and the learning rate is throttled. GrowthBook, Statsig, and Eppo all support CUPED natively.

rule.statement: Set a minimum runtime of 14 days for engagement metrics and at least one full business cycle for revenue metrics. Block winner calls before the minimum.
rule.rationale: Novelty and primacy effects inflate early positive signals. 7-day reads systematically over-estimate long-run impact on habit-forming features.

rule.statement: Always include latency, error rate, and a revenue or retention metric in guardrails. Engineering wins that tank infra are common silent failures.
rule.rationale: A variant that improves conversion by 2% while increasing p95 latency by 40% is not a ship. Without guardrails the team ships the conversion win and opens a follow-up incident.

rule.statement: After ship, leave a holdout (1–5%) running for 4–8 weeks to catch novelty decay. Document the result in the experiment registry.
rule.rationale: Novelty effects decay. A holdout is the only way to distinguish a real sustained lift from a spike that reverts to baseline after the novelty period.

antipattern.description: Experimentation graveyards: large experiment counts with no learning-extraction step.
antipattern.reason: 100k tests per year without a weekly learning-extraction pass produces a write-only registry. The organizational value of experimentation is in the applied learnings, not the count.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Intake: hypothesis-author (sonnet) → experiment-doc.md + JSON spec. Pre-flight: sample-size-calculator (haiku) + guardrail-mapper (sonnet). Launch: flag-deployer (haiku, gated). Monitor: srm-watcher (haiku, hourly) + metric-watcher (haiku, daily). Readout: analyst-agent (opus). Library: learning-extractor (opus, weekly). Governance: metric-registry-keeper (sonnet).

rule.statement: Use cheap models (haiku) for collection, hourly checks, and flag operations. Use sonnet for structured authoring. Reserve opus for synthesis and decision recommendations. Never run opus on hourly SRM checks.
rule.rationale: Opus on hourly SRM watch burns 10x tokens for a chi-square computation haiku handles deterministically. Model-cost tiering applies throughout the pipeline.

section.name: recommended-subagents
section.p: Subagent table for the full agentic loop:

list.item: hypothesis-author (sonnet, on intake): Opportunity-solution-tree node, prior data → experiment-doc.md (hypothesis, primary, guardrail, MDE)
sample-size-calculator (haiku, pre-flight): Baseline rate, MDE, α, power → Required N, expected runtime, traffic-split
guardrail-mapper (sonnet, pre-flight): Surface (checkout, signup, etc.), metric registry → Guardrail metrics + degradation thresholds
flag-deployer (haiku, on approval): Vendor (LaunchDarkly/Statsig/GrowthBook), variant config → API call result, flag URL
srm-watcher (haiku, hourly): Assignment table, expected ratio → Pass/fail + chi-square p-value
metric-watcher (haiku, daily): Warehouse query (dbt model), running window → Daily delta + CI; flags on guardrail breach
analyst-agent (opus, at decision point): All experiment data, segment cuts → Readout: ship/iterate/kill + caveats
learning-extractor (opus, weekly): Closed experiments since last run → Cross-cutting learnings memo
metric-registry-keeper (sonnet, on metric change): dbt models, semantic layer → Diff alert if metric SQL changes mid-flight

section.p: Cheap models (haiku) for collection, hourly checks, and flag operations. Sonnet for structured authoring (docs, mappings). Opus only for synthesis and decision recommendations. Never run opus on hourly SRM watch — it burns tokens for a chi-square test.

example.code: <role>You are the {agent}. Experimentation at scale (Kohavi/Tang/Xu).</role> <inputs> <experiment_id>{exp_yyyy-NN-slug}</experiment_id> <doc>{path to experiment-doc.md}</doc> <metrics>{path to metric-catalog.yaml}</metrics> <data>{warehouse SQL or vendor API result}</data> <prior>{related experiments registry slice}</prior> </inputs> <rules> - Never declare a winner without: lift, CI, p-value, pre-registered primary metric, SRM passed. - Surface novelty risk if runtime < 14 days or primary metric is engagement. - Reject solution-shaped hypotheses. Require: if X then metric M moves by delta. - Never compute on >1 primary metric without Bonferroni correction. - Output JSON to schema {schema_path}; markdown digest <= 80 lines. - Flag interference risk (marketplace, social, viral) and block decision if cluster randomization is required but unavailable. </rules> <task>{cadence-specific instruction}</task>

antipattern.description: Agent paraphrasing confidence intervals as "95% chance of winning."
antipattern.reason: That is a Bayesian posterior, not a frequentist CI. Force the agent to copy vendor-emitted statistics verbatim; never let it re-state them in its own words.

antipattern.description: Hypothesis-author producing "we should redesign onboarding" instead of a behavioral prediction.
antipattern.reason: Solution-shaped hypotheses cannot be falsified. Constrain to Hypothesis-If-Then-Because schema with a numeric MDE field.

antipattern.description: Metric-watcher posting "trending positive" messages that humans interpret as ship signals.
antipattern.reason: Label every non-decisional daily update explicitly: "Non-decisional; sequential testing not enabled. Minimum runtime not reached." Prevents premature ships.

antipattern.description: Learning-extractor emitting a learning bullet from a single null result.
antipattern.reason: Require N >= 5 closed experiments sharing a theme before emitting a learning bullet. Single results are evidence, not learnings.

antipattern.description: API keys for LaunchDarkly/Statsig inlined in the model context.
antipattern.reason: Route credentials through the host environment, never into the model context window. Credentials in context leak to logs, caches, and prompt libraries.

antipattern.description: Flag-deployer creating duplicate experiments on retry.
antipattern.reason: Require an idempotency key (hash of experiment-id) on every vendor API call. Retries without idempotency keys create double-counted exposure events.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM) is the silent killer; without an automated chi-square check on assignment counts, ~6% of experiments will report bogus winners. Peeking and early stopping inflate false-positive rate from 5% to 30%+; sequential tests (Always-Valid Inference, mSPRT) require careful framework support — most ad-hoc setups don't have it. Multiple-comparison explosion: 10 metrics × 50 variants/quarter ≈ 500 tests at α=0.05 ⇒ 25 false wins by chance. Need Bonferroni/BH or pre-registered primary metrics. Novelty + primacy effects: 7-day tests miss 4-week behavior change; agents that auto-call winners at day 7 will systematically misjudge habit-forming features. Metric definition drift: same name, different SQL, different tools — agents synthesizing across Amplitude + Snowflake will silently double-count. Network and interference effects (marketplaces, social features) break SUTVA; A/B becomes biased. Need cluster randomization or switchback designs that most tools don't support. LLMs hallucinate "statistical significance" from CI overlap or p-value misreads — never let an agent declare a winner without explicit framework output (lift, CI, p-value, power, SRM-check). Experimentation graveyards: 100k tests/year sounds heroic but most generate no learning; without a learning-extraction step the registry becomes write-only.

section.name: services-and-platforms
section.p: GrowthBook: OSS + cloud, yes (full REST + SDK). Developer-first, OSS self-host, warehouse-native, supports CUPED + sequential testing. Statsig: SaaS, yes (Console API). Enterprise scale 1T+ events/day, generous free tier, built-in CUPED + Pulse readouts. Eppo: SaaS, yes (REST). Warehouse-native, statistical rigor, dbt-first, no in-app SDK overhead. Amplitude Experiment: SaaS, yes (REST). Tight coupling with Amplitude analytics; weak as standalone flag system. Optimizely: SaaS, yes (REST). Legacy enterprise; expensive; agent integration ok but verbose. LaunchDarkly: SaaS, yes (REST + Terraform provider). Best-in-class flag management; experimentation as add-on. PostHog: OSS + cloud, yes (REST). All-in-one OSS analytics + flags + experiments; weakest stats engine of the set. Split.io: SaaS, yes (REST). Enterprise flags + experimentation; data-streams to warehouse. Convert.com: SaaS, partial. Marketing-A/B; weaker programmatic API. VWO: SaaS, partial. Marketing-A/B; client-side bias. Mixpanel Experiments: SaaS, yes (REST). Tied to Mixpanel analytics. Snowflake / BigQuery / Databricks: DW, yes (SQL). Source of truth; warehouse-native tools (Eppo, Statsig Warehouse Native) read directly. dbt: Modeling, yes (CLI + Cloud API). Define dim_experiment_exposure + metric models once, reused by all agents. Cube / MetricFlow: Semantic layer, yes (REST + GraphQL). Single metric definition; agents query here, not raw warehouse.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: growthbook CLI (@growthbook/cli): Manage experiments, features, definitions as code — npm i -g @growthbook/cli. statsig CLI / Console API: Feature gates, experiments, metrics via REST — docs.statsig.com/console-api. ldcli (LaunchDarkly): Feature flags, segments, env management — docs.launchdarkly.com/home/getting-started/ldcli. eppo API (no first-class CLI): Warehouse-native experiment readouts — docs.geteppo.com/reference/api. optimizely CLI/REST: Legacy A/B + flags — docs.developers.optimizely.com. dbt: Define metrics + experiment exposure tables once, reuse everywhere — getdbt.com. cube / metricflow: Semantic layer for metric governance — cube.dev / docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/about-metricflow. posthog CLI: OSS feature flags + experiments + analytics — posthog.com/docs/api. amplitude-cli: Pull cohort + funnel deltas as JSON — amplitude.com/docs/apis. gh: Treat experiment-doc.md as PR; review gate before launch — cli.github.com. cron / systemd timers: Drive hourly/daily watchers — system. slack webhook / tg-send: Alert SRM and guardrail breaches — NERO ~/bin/tg-send.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Pre-register the primary metric, MDE, and decision rule before traffic flips — agents must reject any experiment-doc lacking these fields. Run an A/A test on the platform quarterly; if false-positive rate ≠ ~5%, fix the platform before shipping verdicts from it. Use CUPED (Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Existing Data) for variance reduction — cuts sample size 30–50% on heavy-tailed metrics; supported by GrowthBook, Statsig, Eppo. Always include latency, error rate, and a revenue/retention metric in guardrails — engineering wins that tank infra are common silent failures. Stratify randomization on known high-variance dimensions (device, country, plan tier) to control for imbalance. Use a metric registry (dbt + Cube/MetricFlow) so "conversion" has one SQL definition; agents must read from registry, never re-derive. Lock the analysis plan; freeze metrics + segments at launch; new cuts after launch are exploratory only and must be tagged as such. Treat sequential testing as opt-in and document the procedure (mSPRT, group-sequential); ad-hoc peeking is the dominant cause of bad ship decisions. After ship, leave a holdout (1–5%) on for 4–8 weeks to catch novelty decay. Quarterly: publish a "tests we got wrong" memo; tracking false ships is the single highest-leverage learning loop. Token budget: cap analyst-agent at 50k tokens per readout; if it needs more, the experiment has too many variants — split it.

section.name: experiment-doc-schema
section.p: Experiment spec schema (experiment-doc.yaml): id (exp_2026-04-checkout-cta), status (draft | review | running | analyzing | shipped | killed), hypothesis (If-Then-Because structure with numeric MDE), primary_metric (checkout_completion_rate), guardrail_metrics (with threshold descriptions), secondary_metrics (exploratory), mde_relative (e.g. 0.02 for 2% lift), power (0.8), alpha (0.05), required_n_per_arm, expected_runtime_days, traffic_split ({control: 0.5, variant: 0.5}), randomization_unit (user_id), exposure_event (viewed_checkout), exclusions (bot_traffic, internal_ip), segment_cuts (device, country, paid_vs_organic), sequential_test (false), cluster_randomization (false), related_experiments, owner, reviewer.

section.name: sample-size-calculator
section.p: Two-proportion z-test sample size helper: calculates required N per arm given baseline rate, minimum detectable effect (MDE), alpha, and power. Also computes expected runtime in days: ceil(n_per_arm / (daily_traffic * split)). Uses statsmodels.stats.power NormalIndPower solver. Runtime is a function of traffic volume and randomization split — warehouse-native shops running Eppo/Statsig can pre-compute runtime estimates before launch.

section.name: srm-check-validation
section.p: Chi-square Sample Ratio Mismatch validation: observed counts vs expected split. Passes if p > 0.001. SRM is a hard gate that must complete before analyst-agent runs. ~6% of experiments have SRM silently without automated checks. An experiment with SRM produces bogus winner declarations that persist in institutional memory.

section.name: integration-patterns
section.p: All agents read/write under .aidocs/product_docs/experiments/ with a per-experiment folder (exp_yyyy-NN-slug/). Human-in-the-loop gates: hypothesis approval, flag deployment, decision sign-off. Wire through Claude Agent SDK with structured outputs (Pydantic/Zod). Pin model output to vendor-emitted statistics — do not let the LLM compute p-values; have it read them from GrowthBook/Statsig/Eppo APIs. Cron schedule for the loop: */15 * * * * for SRM watch, 0 */6 * * * for metric watch, 0 8 * * 1 for weekly readout, 0 9 1 * * for learning memo.

section.name: references
section.p: Ron Kohavi, Diane Tang, Ya Xu — Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments (2020), the canonical reference. Microsoft Experimentation Platform (ExP) — public papers on SRM, CUPED, organizational scaling. GrowthBook docs — docs.growthbook.io (CUPED, sequential testing, warehouse mode). Statsig Pulse + Warehouse Native — docs.statsig.com. Eppo — docs.geteppo.com (warehouse-native, dbt-first). LaunchDarkly Experimentation — docs.launchdarkly.com/home/about-experimentation. Booking.com, Airbnb, Spotify, LinkedIn experimentation engineering blogs (2022–2026). Anthropic Claude Agent SDK — agents, structured outputs, scheduled triggers. Always Valid Inference (Optimizely 2015), Peeking at A/B Tests (Johari et al, 2017) — sequential-testing foundations.

template.purpose: Per-experiment spec: hypothesis, primary metric, guardrails, MDE, traffic split
Two-proportion z-test sample size + runtime calculator
